messages.content:
```tex
\section{Introduction}
Large language models (LLMs) and multimodal large language models (MLLMs) have achieved remarkable progress on ``mental'' work that is primarily language-based—editing and writing, document understanding, and even challenging STEM problem solving. However, performance still lags behind humans on tasks that require sustained interaction with the world: perceiving rich multimodal signals, building an implicit world model over time, using physical or virtual tools (e.g., embodied manipulation, GUI/terminal operations), and conducting long-horizon exploration and planning with feedback.

This gap is particularly evident in open-world vision and agentic settings, where straightforward scaling has not yet produced the same predictable gains seen in language. At the same time, real-world workloads increasingly demand long-horizon capabilities—long videos and streaming perception, long documents and cross-session memory, and multi-step tool use with verification and self-correction. These tasks are abundant, practical, and naturally suited to scalable computation, motivating a shift from short-turn multimodal QA toward longer-context reasoning and decision-making.

We introduce Multimodal Contextual Reasoning (MCR), an efficient formulation for \textit{agentifying} existing foundation models to solve long-horizon problems involving perception, planning, tool use, self-reflection, and—when needed—memorization. MCR unifies the key ingredients of agentic behavior within a single shared context: multimodal observations, task instructions, available tools, intermediate reasoning states, and tool feedback. Importantly, MCR supports a recursive reasoning process: at each step, the model conditions on accumulated multimodal evidence and prior decisions, selects actions (including tool calls), interprets feedback, and updates its internal plan until completion.

A central obstacle to MCR is \emph{context scalability}. Agentic rollouts naturally accumulate long traces, while multimodal perception (especially video) expands the token budget dramatically. Naively increasing context length raises two coupled costs: (i) quadratic attention compute and (ii) a linearly growing KV cache that becomes the dominant inference memory footprint. Prior work has mostly addressed this by \emph{reducing token count} (e.g., sampling/merging/pruning frames or patches). While effective, aggressive token reduction can suppress fine-grained spatiotemporal cues that are often necessary for tool verification, temporal grounding, and multi-step evidence accumulation.

To make MCR practical, we combine two \emph{orthogonal} efficiency levers that preserve agentic faithfulness under long horizons:
(1) \textbf{token-number slimming} via hierarchical spatiotemporal compression (HiCo), a proven long-video token reduction strategy used in our prior InternVideo2.5 system; and
(2) \textbf{token-dimension slimming} via \textbf{M$^2$LA}, which compresses the per-token KV representation inside the LLM attention blocks without discarding tokens.
HiCo primarily reduces \emph{how many} visual tokens enter the reasoning context; M$^2$LA reduces \emph{how expensive each token is to remember} during autoregressive inference. Together, they unlock substantially longer multimodal contexts for agentic rollouts under fixed hardware budgets, enabling deeper exploration and more reliable multi-step reasoning.

To train such agents without expensive continued pretraining, we propose a lightweight multi-phase recipe:
(1) multimodal context extension via supervised fine-tuning to build long-context competence and structured trajectories;
(2) test-time scaling reinforcement learning to improve multi-step decision-making and robustness under long horizons; and
(3) a small amount of preference optimization to balance newly acquired agentic skills with the base model’s inherited capabilities.
Empirically, this approach enables existing MLLMs to reach a new level on long-horizon tasks, including long-video understanding and temporal grounding, long-document comprehension, and tool-based agent manipulation.

\section{Method}
\paragraph{Formulation.} We formulate MCR as a closed-loop, multi-step rollout where an MLLM iteratively performs reasoning, chooses tool/perception/memory actions, receives feedback from the environment, and updates a shared multimodal context until termination, as follows:
\begin{align}
\mathbf{R}_i \sim f_{\theta}\!\left(\mathbf{Q}\ \middle|\ \mathbf{V}_i,\ \mathbf{A}_i,\ \mathbf{F}_i,\ \mathbf{C}_i\right)_{|i=1,2,\dots,N},
\qquad 
\text{i.f.f.}\qquad 
\mathbf{R}_N \models \mathbf{Q},
\label{eq:mcr_main}
\\[1mm]
\text{w.r.t.}\quad 
\mathbf{F}_{i+1} \sim \pi\!\left(\mathbf{A}_i\ \middle|\ \mathbf{R}_i,\mathbf{Q}\right),\ 
\mathbf{V}_{i+1} \sim \delta\!\left(\mathbf{V}_i\ \middle|\ \mathbf{R}_i,\mathbf{F}_{i+1}\right),\ 
\mathbf{A}_{i+1} \sim \rho\!\left(\mathbf{A}_i\ \middle|\ \mathbf{R}_i,\mathbf{F}_{i+1},\mathbf{Q}\right),
\label{eq:mcr_transitions}
\\[1mm]
\mathbf{C}_{i+1} = \mathbf{C}_i \oplus \left(\mathbf{R}_i,\mathbf{A}_i,\mathbf{F}_{i+1}\right),
\qquad 
\mathbf{C}_1 = \mathbf{T}_{\texttt{sys}},
\qquad 
(\mathbf{V}_1,\mathbf{A}_1,\mathbf{F}_1)=(\mathbf{V}_{\texttt{init}},\varnothing,\varnothing),
\label{eq:mcr_context}
\end{align}
where $f_\theta(\cdot)$ is the MLLM policy that produces an intermediate reasoning/result $\mathbf{R}_i$ given the task $\mathbf{Q}$, current perception $\mathbf{V}_i$ (image/video or a focused temporal/spatial window), tool/action trace $\mathbf{A}_i$, tool/environment feedback $\mathbf{F}_i$, and accumulated context $\mathbf{C}_i$. 
$\pi(\cdot)$ denotes execution of the current tool-use/action $\mathbf{A}_i$ in an environment and returns feedback $\mathbf{F}_{i+1}$ (e.g., retrieved evidence, program output, GUI state, error logs). 
$\delta(\cdot)$ updates perception by selecting the next informative multimodal evidence conditioned on the current reasoning and feedback (e.g., re-sampling frames, temporal zoom-in, spatial crop). 
$\rho(\cdot)$ updates tool-use decisions (including tool invocation, memory read/write, verification, or termination-related actions) based on the evolving reasoning and feedback. 
$\oplus$ aggregates the agentic trace into the shared context (optionally with summarization/compression) to support long-horizon memory and self-reflection. 
The rollout continues until the final step $N$ yields a response satisfying the request, $\mathbf{R}_N \models \mathbf{Q}$.

\subsection{Dynamic Token \& Dimension Slimming for Long-Context Multimodality}
Long-horizon MCR requires repeatedly appending multimodal observations and intermediate traces into $\mathbf{C}_i$. For videos, this produces token inflation at two levels: (i) \emph{many} spatiotemporal tokens (token-count pressure) and (ii) expensive per-token KV states in the LLM (token-dimension pressure). We address both with complementary slimming mechanisms:
\emph{HiCo} reduces the number of visual tokens that enter the LLM, while \emph{M$^2$LA} reduces the KV-cache footprint per token after tokens have entered the LLM. These two mechanisms are orthogonal and can be used jointly in MCR rollouts.

\paragraph{Multimodal Multi-head Latent Attention.}
We propose \textbf{M$^2$LA} to make the LLM backbone memory-efficient under long multimodal contexts by compressing the cached key/value states in attention. Concretely, M$^2$LA replaces standard GQA-style attention blocks with a \emph{latent} KV representation: instead of storing full per-head keys/values for every token, we store a compact latent vector per token and reconstruct head-specific keys/values on the fly.

\textbf{Reparameterizing pretrained attention.}
Starting from a pretrained GQA block, M$^2$LA performs a training-light conversion into a latent-attention form while preserving behavior on short contexts:
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{RoPE-aware positional aggregation.} For channels that carry rotary positional information, we aggregate multi-head positional keys into a shared representation (MQA-style) by learning a position-compatible linear aggregation from a small calibration pass. The goal is to preserve the attention score geometry induced by RoPE while avoiding caching redundant positional channels per head.
    \item \textbf{Low-rank latent factorization for content channels.} For non-positional (content) channels, we introduce a low-rank bottleneck: keys and values are projected into a compact latent space (cached), and head/group-specific keys/values are produced by learned up-projections. This decouples KV-cache size from the number of heads/groups and from the original per-head dimension.
\end{itemize}

\textbf{Modality-aware latent adaptation.}
A single latent space must serve both text and vision tokens, which have notably different feature statistics. M$^2$LA therefore equips the latent pathway with lightweight modality-aware adapters (e.g., separate affine/linear maps for text vs.\ vision tokens) applied on the cached latent vectors before reconstruction. This allows the model to keep a shared attention interface while specializing the latent representation to multimodal distributions.

\textbf{Dynamic latent budgeting.}
In long multimodal sequences, the marginal utility of storing high-rank visual KV states can vary across depth and along the rollout: early layers may need richer visual details for alignment and retrieval, while later layers increasingly rely on distilled linguistic states and recent tool feedback. M$^2$LA exploits this by allowing \emph{layer-wise and/or modality-wise} latent dimensions, shrinking the cached latent rank where it is less beneficial (e.g., deeper layers for visual tokens) without dropping any tokens. This yields a controllable memory--accuracy trade-off that is particularly suitable for agentic contexts that grow over time.

Overall, M$^2$LA can be viewed as \emph{dimension slimming}: it keeps the full token stream required for faithful agentic reasoning, while substantially reducing KV cache memory, enabling longer MCR rollouts and larger perception windows under the same hardware budget.

\paragraph{Hierarchical Compression for Spatiotemporal Tokens.}
While M$^2$LA reduces the cost of caching tokens, long videos can still produce an excessive number of visual tokens. We therefore also employ \textbf{HiCo}, a hierarchical video token compression scheme introduced and validated in our previous InternVideo2.5 work. In this paper, HiCo serves as the \emph{token slimming} component that reduces redundancy before the LLM context is formed.

HiCo compresses video tokens in two stages:
(1) \textbf{clip-level compression} segments long videos into clips and uses spatiotemporal encoding plus similarity-based merging to remove inter-frame redundancy while retaining salient motion/appearance cues; and
(2) \textbf{video-level compression} further reduces tokens inside the LLM by progressively discarding less relevant visual tokens as the model goes deeper, leveraging the empirical sparsity pattern that shallow layers attend broadly while deeper layers concentrate on task-relevant moments.

In MCR, HiCo can be applied adaptively via $\delta(\cdot)$: the agent may start with coarse compressed context and selectively expand or re-sample specific temporal segments when tool feedback or intermediate reasoning indicates missing evidence. Importantly, because HiCo and M$^2$LA operate on different axes (token count vs.\ per-token KV dimension), combining them yields compounded savings and makes long-horizon multimodal rollouts feasible in practice.

\section{Implementation Details}
\label{sec:impl_details}
We summarize key implementation choices for integrating M$^2$LA and HiCo into the MCR agent training and inference stack.

\paragraph{Backbone and multimodal inputs.}
Our agent backbone is an instruction-tuned MLLM with interleaved multimodal tokens in a single causal sequence. Video inputs are converted to spatiotemporal tokens by a video-capable visual encoder and a connector that maps visual features into the LLM embedding space. The agent context $\mathbf{C}_i$ is constructed by interleaving (i) multimodal observations, (ii) tool specifications and tool outputs, and (iii) the agent’s intermediate reasoning traces, following Eq.~\ref{eq:mcr_context}.

\paragraph{M$^2$LA conversion and calibration.}
We apply M$^2$LA as a drop-in replacement for attention blocks in the LLM backbone:
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Block conversion.} Each GQA block is reparameterized into a latent-attention form where cached states consist of (a) a compact latent KV vector and (b) a shared positional (RoPE) component.
    \item \textbf{Calibration pass.} We run a small calibration set that covers representative mixtures of text, images, and videos to collect attention activations. Using these activations, we fit (i) the RoPE aggregation mapping for positional channels and (ii) low-rank projections/up-projections for non-positional channels via least-squares-style regression. This step is fast and does not require full continued pretraining.
    \item \textbf{Recovery tuning.} After conversion, we optionally perform a brief SFT-style recovery tuning to stabilize response style and multimodal alignment. We keep this stage lightweight by updating only a small subset of parameters (e.g., connectors/adapters and, when used, modality-aware latent adapters), while freezing the visual encoder.
\end{itemize}

\paragraph{Modality-aware adapters and dynamic latent ranks.}
We attach modality-aware adapters on the latent pathway to account for distribution shifts between text and visual tokens. Adapters are initialized to near-identity to preserve the original model behavior and are refined during recovery tuning / downstream post-training.
For dynamic latent budgeting, we allow layer-wise latent dimensions (and optionally different budgets for text vs.\ vision tokens). In practice, we specify a per-layer schedule (e.g., decreasing visual latent rank with depth) and validate it using held-out multimodal likelihood signals or task proxies, then fix the schedule during training and inference.

\paragraph{HiCo token compression.}
We reuse the HiCo implementation from our InternVideo2.5 pipeline with minimal changes:
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Clip-level.} Videos are segmented into clips; clip features are produced with spatiotemporal attention in the video encoder, followed by similarity-based token merging to reduce redundancy.
    \item \textbf{Video-level.} Inside the LLM, we apply progressive visual dropout: shallow layers preserve broad coverage with light uniform dropping, while deeper layers keep the most instruction-relevant tokens using text-guided relevance.
\end{itemize}
HiCo is applied before forming $\mathbf{V}_i$ and can be adjusted by $\delta(\cdot)$ (e.g., higher fidelity on segments flagged by tool feedback).

\paragraph{Systems optimizations for long contexts.}
For training and inference with long multimodal sequences, we employ standard long-context engineering:
(i) sequence parallelism and data packing to improve throughput,
(ii) gradient checkpointing to reduce activation memory,
and (iii) prefill optimizations for multimodal prompts to amortize long-prefix compute.
M$^2$LA primarily reduces KV-cache memory during autoregressive decoding, which directly benefits agentic rollouts that repeatedly call tools and generate intermediate traces.

\paragraph{Summary of roles in MCR.}
Within Eq.~\ref{eq:mcr_main}--\ref{eq:mcr_context}, HiCo mainly improves the efficiency of $\mathbf{V}_i$ construction (token-count control), while M$^2$LA reduces the memory cost of conditioning $f_\theta$ on the ever-growing shared context $\mathbf{C}_i$ (token-dimension control). This combination enables longer rollouts and richer perception under fixed compute/memory budgets, which is critical for agentifying MLLMs in long-horizon multimodal environments.
```
